text: Hi, Dr. Charles Chabert, urologist and director of The Prostate Clinic. The following video is the first in a series of videos highlighting the various steps used in a robotic assisted radical prostatectomy.
The surgeon gains access from the intraperitoneal cavity into the preperitoneal space by dividing the uracil attachment from the umbilicus, creating the preperitoneal plane.
The surgeon continues the dissection using a combination of sharp and blunt techniques, focusing on the lateral aspect just posterior to the medial umbilical ligament.
The surgeon continues dissection using bipolar forceps in the left hand and monopolar scissors in the right hand, manipulating tissue within the surgical field.
Coordination between the assistant and the primary surgeon is critical to facilitate good and prompt access into the retropubic space of retzius.

The surgeon visualizes the pelvic floor and mobilizes the right prevesical space, progressing towards the medial umbilical ligament and descending into the retropubic space of Retzius on the patient's right side.
The surgeon identifies and manipulates loose areolar tissue, which appears relatively avascular, facilitating straightforward access towards the pelvic floor.

The shiny tissue visible on the right side is the reflection of the endopelvic fascia. The puboprostatic ligament, seen as a condensation on both sides, secures the prostate to the posterior aspect of the pubis. The surgeon proceeds to defat the prostate, noting the patient's relative paucity of extraperitoneal or preperitoneal fat.
The surgeon removes tissue to enhance visualization of the anterior surface of the prostate, highlighting the junction between the prostate and the bladder.

The surgeon makes an incision in the endopelvic fascia on the patient's right side, using sharp and blunt dissection to reflect the fibers of the levator ani muscle. These fibers are retracted and dissected off, extending around to the apex of the prostate.
The puboprostatic ligament is visible at the top of the screen, with the prostatic component being divided while the urethral component remains intact.
My opinion on this is that it facilitates better support of the urethra and allows earlier return of urinary control.
The surgeon performs sharp and blunt dissection on the patient's left side, reflecting the fibers of levator ani off the endopelvic fascia and retracting them to expose the apex of the prostate.
The surgeon uses diathermy to carefully access the endopelvic fascia, reflecting the levator ani fibers off the prostate with a combination of sharp and blunt dissection.
The surgeon cauterizes a small perforating bleeder from the levator ani muscle using bipolar diathermy, ensuring hemostasis in the surgical field.
The surgeon divides the superficial component of the puboprostatic ligament on the patient's left side, while leaving the urethral aspect intact.

The surgeon releases the cephalad retraction on the bladder, securing the cut edges of the reflected endopelvic fascia over the prostate in the midline, which aids in controlling the dorsal venous complex.

The surgeon uses an O PDS suture on a semicircular needle, passing it through the notch between the urethra and the dorsal venous complex (DVC) to the contralateral side. The absence of bleeding upon pulling the suture confirms correct placement, and the suture is secured on the preserved urethral component.

The surgeon secures the puboprostatic ligament with a stitch, ensuring hemostasis during apical dissection. A slip knot is applied with alternating throws, elevating the suture at ninety degrees to the urethral plane. The knot is cinched down in a lassoing fashion to control the dorsal venous complex (DVC).
The surgeon secures the suture with additional throws to prevent it from pulling through or slipping during the procedure.
The surgeon divides the suture, removes the needle, and places a second suture in the surgical field.
The surgeon places a suture through the previously divided uracus, providing additional cephalad retraction of the bladder to facilitate the dissection of the bladder neck.

If you enjoyed this video, please give it a thumbs up and subscribe to our YouTube channel for more content on the contemporary management of prostate disease. Thanks again, and if you have any questions, please leave them in the dialogue box below.
